Facts.text: Roman General Gaius Carrinas' triumphant procession through Rome, awarded for fighting in Gaul
Dedication of the Mat House / public meeting house at the Maya City of Copán under ruler Smoke Monkey
An insurrection delivers Paris to the Burgundians
Magdalen College, Oxford founded by William Waynflete, Bishop of Winchester
Frisian rebel leader Jancko Douwama arrested
Turkish admiral Chaireddin "Barbarossa" allows Giulia Gonzaga to kidnap and plunder in Naples
Land guardians of Netherlands attack Verdun
- 13] First Anglo-Dutch War: Battle of the Gabbard/ Battle at North Foreland, English fleet beats the Dutch
England installs a municipal government in New York City
New Amsterdam legally becomes an English colony and is renamed New York after the English Duke of York
Raid on the Medway: Dutch fleet sails up the Medway destroying British naval ships and capturing the flagship HMS Royal Charles
French army under General Turenne crosses the Rhine at Lobith during the Dutch War
Pope Innocent XII succeeds Alexander VIII
Prussia and Russia sign secret treaty
Patent for the 'Spinning Jenny', the first multiple spinning machine that revolutionizes cotton spinning, is granted to English weaver James Hargreaves
Marion du Fresne is killed at Tacoury's Cove, Bay of Islands, New Zealand, by local Māori
1st naval battle of Revolution-Unity (US) captures Margaretta (Br)
Virginia adopts Declaration of Rights
Delegates to the Constitutional Convention (held in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania) agree to requirement providing a senator must be at least 30 years old
British explorer Captain George Vancouver lands on the site of what is now Vancouver, British Columbia
Dutch colonial troops driven out of Palembang, Sumatra
Beginning of French colonization of Algeria: 34,000 French soldiers land 27 kilometers west of Algiers at Sidi Ferruch
Hopkins Observatory, dedicated in Williamstown, Massachusetts
Iowa Territory forms with Burlington as its capital
Meteorite hits Uden, Netherlands
George Abernethy becomes 1st governor of Oregon Country
Gas mask patented by Lewis Haslett (Louisville, Kentucky)
Abass I of Egypt signs a contract with Robert Louis Stevenson to build a railway in Egypt, first stage opens 1854 between Alexandria and Kafer Eassa - first railway in Africa
Comstock Silver Lode in Nevada discovered, first major silver discovery in the US
The State Bank of the Russian Empire is established

Missouri Governor Claiborne Jackson calls for 50,000 volunteers to stop Federal "invasion" from taking over his state after US Army forces led by Nathaniel Lyon arrest pro-Confederate state militia at Fort Jackson
Battle of Trevilian Station, the bloodiest and largest all-cavalry battle of the American Civil War, ends with a Confederate tactical victory and a Union successful retreat in Louisa County, Virginia
Skirmish at Mcafee's Cross Road, Georgia, about 57 die in battle
Worcester Ruby Legs pitcher Lee Richmond throws the first perfect game in MLB history in a 1-0 win over the Cleveland Blues at the Agricultural County Fair Grounds in Worcester
The USS Jeannette, under the command of George Washington De Long, is crushed by Arctic ice after 21 months of ice-bound drifting
Roof collapse kills 30 at murder trial in France
88 people are killed in the Armagh rail disaster, Ireland
Netherlands Society for Currency & Coin collecting forms

England cricket spin bowler Jack Hearne sets record for earliest date taking 100 first-class wickets in a season when he captures 9-73 for the MCC in an innings & 18 run drubbing of Australia at Lord's
Possibly the most severe quake in history strikes Assam, India, Shock waves felt over an area size of Europe (low mortality rate given size of earthquake, 1,500 casualties).
New Richmond Tornado: the eighth deadliest tornado in U.S. history kills 117 peoples and injures around 200
In Germany, the Reichstag pass the second Navy Bill, which calls for doubling the German Navy within 20 years
In Cuba, the constitutional convention - knowing that the USA will not withdraw its troops until does so - adopts the Platt Amendment as part of its constitution
Australian parliament agrees to Commonwealth Franchise Act, granting female suffrage
Horn & Hardart open the first automat, where food and drink are served through vending machines, in the US at 818 Chestnut St in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Niagara Falls, Ontario incorporated as a city
The Sigma Alpha Iota International Music Fraternity is founded at the University of Michigan School of Music
Yanks commit 11 errors & lose 14-6 to Tigers
Lusitania crosses Atlantic in record 4 days 15 hours (NYC)
"Shine On, Harvest Moon" by Ada Jones & Billy Murray hits #1
Dutch soccer club PEC forms in Zwolle; renamed FC Zwolle in 1990 and back to PEC Zwolle in 2012
"Dachshund" by Pathe Freres, early animated cartoon, released
US Secret Service extends protection of the President to include his family
1st aerial bombing raid by an American unit, France
Dutch 2nd Chamber accord for equal Christian-public education
Farmer Labor Party organized (Chicago)
St Louis Cardinals make MLB record 10 straight hits in 6th inning of a 14-8 win over the Phillies at the Baker Bowl, Philadelphia
Brazil leaves League of Nations
Financial and Economy World conference opens (66 countries)
Weapons pact between Bolivia and Paraguay ends the three-year-old Chaco War
1st 50 KW US radio station (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania)
LPGA Western Open Women's Golf, Topeka CC: Opal Hill scores a 3 & 2 win over Mrs. Charkes Dennehy to claim her second consecutive major title
Baseball Hall of Fame is dedicated in Cooperstown, New York
Shooting begins on Paramount Pictures' "Dr. Cyclops," the first horror film photographed in three-strip Technicolor
Tornado kills 35 in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma
British 12th airborne battalion and the 13th & 18th Hussars attack and capture Bréville
Churchill/Marshall/Arnold visit Montgomery's HQ in Chateau de Creully
US troop march into Carentan/Caumont, Normandy
WWII: US 7th Marine regiment conquers summit of Kunishi Ridge, Okinawa
British Amateur Women's Golf, Gullane GC: Babe Didrikson becomes first American to win event; beats England's Jacqueline Gordon 5 & 4
2 Air France DC-4s crash near Bahrain, about 100 die
USSR declares peace treaty with Japan invalid
Milwaukee Braves spot starting pitcher Jim Wilson throws first no-hitter in history of County Stadium when he blanks Philadelphia Phillies, 2-0
Monitor (full weekend program) begins on NBC radio network
TV comedy "Mister Peepers" starring Wally Cox airs for last time on NBC
"Make Me Laugh" TV Game Show last airs on ABC-TV, syndicated 1979
KORN (now KDLT) TV channel 5 in Mitchell-Sioux Falls, SD (ABC) begins
Dutch Lockheed Electricity "Sirius" accident at Cairo, kills 20
Laos Prince Souvanna Phouma forms coalition government
USAF Maj Robert M White takes X-15 to 56,270 m
Morio Shigematsu of Japan runs world record marathon (2:12:00) in the Polytechnic Marathon in London, England
Queen Elizabeth's Birthday Honours List names The Beatles as MBEs (Members of the Most Excellent Order of the British Empire); dozens of past winners return medals in protest
South Vietnam Gen Nguyen Cao Ky succeeds Phan Huy Quat as premier
The Big Bang theory of the creation of the universe is supported by the announcement of the discovery of new celestial bodies known as blue galaxies
In the case of Loving v. Virginia, United States Supreme Court unanimously strikes down laws against interracial marriages
Race riot in Cincinnati Ohio (300 arrested)
USSR launches Venera 4 for parachute landing on Venus
Washington Senators beat Chicago White Sox, 6-5 in 22 innings at D.C. Stadium
MLB Pittsburgh Pirates pitcher Doc Ellis no-hits the Padres, 2-0 at San Diego Stadium; he later claimed to be under the influence of LSD at the time
Coleraine bombings: six Protestant civilians were killed and 33 wounded by a Provisional Irish Republican Army car bomb in Coleraine, County Londonderry
New Zealand all-rounder Bevan Congdon scores 176 as Kiwis chase 479 in 1st Test v England at Nottingham; tourists just miss out, dismissed for 440
Yanks trade wife swapper Mike Kekich for Lowell Palmer
Oakland A's outfielder Billy Williams hits his 400th career HR
David Berkowitz sentenced in NY Supreme Court to 25 yrs to life
US House of Representatives allows live radio coverage
Bryan Allen flies a human-powered aircraft, the Gossamer Albatross over English Channel, taking 2 hrs, 49 min
Kevin St Onge throws a playing card a record 185 ft-1 inch; record broken in 1992
Sandinista occupies parts of Managua
Baseball players begin a 50 day strike, their 3rd strike
Only candidate Hassan Gouled Aptidon wins Djibouti Presidential election
750,000 anti-nuclear demonstrators, rally in Central Park, NYC
Battle of Mount Longdon in the Falkland Islands ends in a British victory
Comet C/1983 (Sugano-Saigusa-Fujikawa) approaches 0.0628 AUs of Earth
US PresidentRonald Reaganchallenges Mikhail Gorbachev to "tear down" the Berlin wall
Andy Hampton is 1st American to win Round of Italy
Republic of Ireland beats England 1-0 at Euro '88 thanks to a headed goal by Ray Houghton in Ireland's first match at a major football tournament
"Doctor Doctor", TV Comedy starring Matt Frewer, debuts on CBS-TV
Canadian Olympian Ben Johnson admits using steroids
Egypt (500-1 longshot) ties favorite the Netherlands 1-1 in World Cup game
NY Mets beat Chicago Cubs 19-8 at Wrigley Field, Cubs outfielder Doug Dazcenzo pitched a scoreless 9th inning
"Three Little Pigs" by Green Jelly hits #17
48th Tony Awards: "Angels in America: Perestroika" (play) & "Passion: (musical) win
Moshood Abiola declares himself lawful President of Nigeria
3 Philadelphia Fed Court judges overturn US indecency ban on internet
Cincinnati Reds CEO Marge Schott gives up day-to-day operations due to numerous insensitive comments about Adolf Hitler, working women and Asians
1st ever baseball regular season inter-league game SF Giants beat Texas Rangers 4-3
Kosovo War: Operation Joint Guardian begins - a NATO-led United Nations peacekeeping force KFor enters the province of Kosovo in Federal Republic of Yugoslavia

Sandro Rosa do Nascimento takes hostages while robbing Bus #174 in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil; the highly-publicized standoff becomes a media circus and ends with the death of do Nascimento and a hostage.
A 1.3 kilogram chondrite type meteorite strikes a house in Ellerslie, New Zealand causing serious damage but no injuries
Ireland rejects the Lisbon Treaty in a referendum, thus putting into question the reform programme of the European Union.
Protests in Iran follow the presidential election
Stanley Cup Final, Joe Louis Arena, Detroit, MI: Pittsburgh Penguins edge Detroit Red Wings, 2-1 for a 4-3 series win; Penguins 3rd Championship
US television broadcasts switch from analog NTSC to digital ATSC transmission
65th Tony Awards: "The Book of Mormon" (musical) & "War Horse" (play) win
An Australian coroner's report rules that a dingo was responsible for the death of a baby in 1980
The chemical compound NOTT-202, which is capable of selectively absorbing carbon dioxide, is created
World Health Organization concludes that diesel exhaust causes cancer
Russia passes a law banning gay propaganda
Israeli leadership blames Hamas after 3 Israeli teenagers are kidnapped in the West Bank
Al-Qaeda's 2nd-in-command Nasser al-Wuhayshi (Osama Bin Laden's former private secretary) is killed in a US air strike in Yemen
Zimbabwe discards its own currency, offering an exchange of $1 for 35 quadrillion Zimbabwean dollars
Gunman claiming allegiance to the Islamic State opens fire at gay nightclub in Orlando, Florida, killing 49, injuring 53 - worst mass shooting in the US
Stanley Cup Final, SAP Center, San Jose, CA: Pittsburgh Penguins defeat San Jose Sharks, 3-1 for a 4-2 series victory; Penguins 4th title
MLB Draft: JSerra Catholic HS, CA shortstop Royce Lewis first pick by Minnesota Twins
AT&T's $85.4 billion takeover of Time Warner receives approval from a US district court judge
Battle for key Yemen port of Hudaydah begins with assault by Saudi backed pro-government forces against Iranian-backed Houthi rebels
French photographer Jean-Claude Arnault, at center of Nobel Prize Committee scandal, charged with rape in Sweden
Earliest evidence of humans smoking cannabis discovered in 2,500-year-old grave in Pamir Mountains, China, published in "Science Advances"
Same-sex marriage approved by Ecuador's Constitutional Court in landmark ruling
Stanley Cup Final, TD Garden, Boston, MA: St. Louis Blues beat Boston Bruins, 4-1 for a 4-3 series victory; first title in franchise history
Violent protests in Hong Kong as tens of thousands of protesters block and try to storm government buildings to stop extradition law
World’s largest sovereign wealth fund, Norway’s Government Pension Fund, worth $1 trillion, gets authorization to drop fossil fuel investments from Norway's government
African American Rayshard Brooks shot dead in drive-through carpark in Atlanta leading to further protests at police violence and the resignation of city's police chief

Danish star soccer midfielder Christian Eriksen suffers an on-field cardiac arrest during Euro 2020 game with Finland in Copenhagen; revived with a defibrillator; game controversially restarts with 1-0 Finland win
75th Tony Awards: held live for 1st time since 2019 - Best New Musical “A Strange Loop”, Best New Play “The Lehman Trilogy”
At least 46 people, including 23 children, killed in the Democratic Republic of Congo at Lala displacement camp by militia group ‘Codeco’
Boat carrying wedding guests overturns killing 106 with 144 rescued on the river Niger in Nigeria's north central Kwara State
MLB Philadelphia Phillies’ catcher J.T. Realmuto hits for the cycle, 1st player for team to do so since 2004, in 9-8 loss to Diamondbacks in Arizona
"The Safekeeping" by Yael van Der Wouden wins the 2025 Women's Prize for Fiction and "The Story of a Heart" by Rachel Clarkes wins the Women's Prize for Non-Fiction

Air India Flight 171, a Boeing 787-8 Dreamliner passenger jet bound for London, crashes moments after takeoff from Ahmedabad, India; all but one of the 242 people aboard and dozens of others on the ground are killed
The National Weather Service offices in Fairbanks and Juneau, Alaska issue first-ever heat advisory, warning of temperatures up to 88°F (31.1°C)
KingAlfonso Vof Aragon occupies Naples
English KingEdward VIaccepts Archbishop Thomas Cranmer's "Forty-two Articles" on church reformation
Battle of Okehazama:Oda Nobunagadefeats Imagawa Yoshimoto in Owari Province, Japan
The newly completed Fontana dei Quattro Fiumi (Fountain of the Four Rivers) byGian Lorenzo Berniniis unveiled in Rome
James II, then Duke of York resigns as Lord High Admiral
Rye Houseplot against English KingCharles IIuncovered
Act of Settlement gives English and Scottish crown to Sophia, Princess of Hanover (as granddaughter of James VI and I of Scotland and England). Her sonGeorge Iinherits in 1714.
Confederate forces of Jubal Early attack Fort Stevens, barely 4 miles from the White House, withAbraham Lincolnobserving the battle
Physicist and mathematicianJohn William Strutt[Lord Raleigh] elected fellow of the Royal Society
Filipino revolutionary forces under GeneralEmilio Aguinaldoproclaimed the sovereignty and independence of the Philippine Islands from the colonial rule of Spain
Browns pitcher Hub Pruett strikes out future Baseball Hall of Fame sluggerBabe Ruth3 consecutive times as St. Louis beats New York Yankees, 7 - 1 at Sportsman's Park, St. Louis
German Reich presidentFriedrich Ebertvisits Munich
Harry Houdinifrees himself from a straight jacket while suspended upside down, 40 feet (12 m) above ground in NYC
New York Yankees future Baseball Hall of Fame first basemanLou Gehrigcollects 14 total bases with 2 triples and 2 home runs in a 15-7 win over Chicago White Sox
German boxerMax Schmelingbeats Jack Sharkey by disqualification in 4 rounds in NYC for vacant NBA, NYSAC, The Ring and lineal heavyweight titles; first time title won on a foul
Al Caponeis indicted on 5,000 counts of prohibition violations and perjury
Black-McKeller Bill passes causes the break-up ofWilliam Boeing's empire into Boeing United Aircraft [Technologies] and United Airlines
SenatorHuey Longof Louisiana speaks continually for 15½ hours in then Senate's longest speech (150,000 words)
After a one-day show trial, eight Soviet generals are sentenced to death and executed by the NKVD
Kwame Nkrumahannounces the formation of Ghana's Convention People's Party (CPP), the word "convention" chosen "to carry the masses with us"
Following his retirement announcement after 49 years as Philadelphia Athletics managerConnie Mackis named Honorary Manager of the MLB All-Star Game
Stan Musialplays in 823rd game (new NL consecutive-game streak)
"Cleopatra" directed by Joseph Mankiewicz and starringElizabeth TaylorandRichard Burtonpremieres in NYC, then most expensive film ever made
American civil rights activistMedgar Eversis assassinated by white supremacistByron De La Beckwithin Jackson, Mississippi
Sonny &Chermake their 1st TV appearance on "American Bandstand"
Dave Clark Five sets record as they appear for 12th time on "TheEd SullivanShow"
"You Only Live Twice", 5th James Bond film starringSean Connery, screenplay byRoald Dahl, premieres in London
U.S. premiere of horror film "Rosemary's Baby", based on the bestselling novel of the same name byIra Levin
Rocker and blues singerJanis Joplindebuts in Kentucky

Apple Records releasesJohn LennonandYoko Ono's political album "Sometime in NYC" in US; includes studio recordings of songs "Woman is the Nigger of the World", "Attica State" and "Luck of the Irish"; and some live tracks from 1971 concert withFrank Zappa
Hank Aaronsmacks his first home run in Milwaukee since 1965 as his new team, the Brewers beat Oakland A's, 9-7
"Yes, Yes, Yes" single byBill Cosbyhits #46
Groundbreaking ceremony forJohn F. KennedyPresidential Library and Museum in Boston, Massachusetts; architectI. M. Pei's design would take over 2 years to build
Detroit Tigers fire manager Les Moss, hiringSparky Andersonwho stays until 1995; includes World Series title 1984 and AL Manager of the Year Award 1984 and 1987
Ronald Reagansays he will submit to periodic medical tests
"Raiders of the Lost Ark" (the first Indiana Jones film) directed bySteven Spielberg, starringHarrison Ford, with story byGeorge Lucaspremieres
Larry HolmesTKOsLeon Spinksin 3 for WBC heavyweight boxing title
P. W. Bothadeclares South African national emergency
Mariah Carey's self-titled debut album is released
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonbecomes 2nd to steal 900 bases
NY Yankee relieverDave Righettibecomes 9th to record 200 saves
OriolesCal Ripkenplays in his 1,308th consecutive game to move past Everett Scott into 2nd place on the all-time list
Boris Yeltsinwins Russia's first presidential election with 57% of the vote
-17] PopeJohn Paul IIvisits Spain
Cab Callowaysuffers a massive stroke at his home in White Plaines NY
Bangladesh Awami League wins 146 of 300 seats in general election, bringingSheikh Hasinato power for her first term as Prime Minister with the support of the Jatiya Party
"Batman and Robin" directed by Joel Schumacher, starringGeorge Clooney,Arnold SchwarzeneggerandChris O'Donnellpremieres in Los Angeles
"X-Men" 1st film based on comic book series, starringPatrick Stewart,Hugh Jackman, andIan McKellenpremieres on Ellis Island in New York Harbor
"Transformers", directed byMichael Bayand starringShia LaBeoufandMegan Foxpremieres in Sydney
Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn, Russian novelist and historian, is awarded the State Prize of the Russian Federation for his humanitarian work byPresident Putin
Coldplayrelease their 4th studio album " Viva la Vida or Death" (winner of 3 Grammys)
Patrick Kanereceives the Calder Memorial Trophy as the NHL's rookie of the year
"Toy Story3," directed by Lee Unkrich and starringTom HanksandTim Allen, premieres at the Taormina Film Fest in Italy - first animated film to earn $1 billion
70th Tony Awards: "Hamilton" (musical) wins 11 awards after record 16 nominations, Leslie Odom Jr.,Cynthia Erivo, Frank Langella,Jessica Langewin acting awards; "The Humans" (play) wins 4
Cristiano Ronaldobecomes 1st footballer to top Forbes' Highest-Paid Athletes List, earning $88 million
Forbes release Top 100 highest-paid entertainers list - Sean "Puff Daddy" Combs at No.1 with earnings of $130 million
Russian opposition leaderAlexei Navalnysentenced to 30 days administrative arrest for organizing rallies
GovernorRon DeSantisdeclares a state of emergency in southern Florida after torrential rains cause flash floods
Peasants' Revolt: English rebels arrive at Blackheath outside London, 14-year-old KingRichard IIretreats to the safety of the Tower of London
Anne Frankreceives her diary as a birthday present in Amsterdam
Nelson Mandelais sentenced to life in prison for sabotage in South Africa
Central African Republic ex-emperorJean-Bédel Bokassasentenced to death
Soviet Union holds its 1st beauty content in Moscow, 16-year-old Maria Kalinina crowned the winner
Shakespeare's Globe theatre opens in London, England, replica of original Globe theatre (1599-1642) with performance of Henry V, after campaign by Sam Wanamaker
Singapore Summit between North Korean leaderKim Jong-unand US President Donald Trump - first time a North Korean leader and an incumbent US President meet
Reizei, 63rd Emperor of Japan (967-9), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1011)
Emperor Gaozong of Song, 10th emperor of Song dynasty, born in China (d. 1187)
Juan del Encina, Spanish author and composer, born in Encinas, Spain (d. 1529)
Cosimo I de Medici, Grand Duke of Tuscany and art patron of Florence, born in Florence (d. 1574)
Paul Guldin, Swiss astronomer and mathematician (Guldinus theorem), born in Mels, Switzerland (d. 1643)
Johan Wolfert van Brederode, Dutch governor (Hertogenbosch) and field marshal, born in Heusden, Noord-Brabant, Netherlands (d. 1655)
Yamamoto Tsunetomo, Japanese samurai of the Saga Domain and Zen Buddhist monk (Hagakure), born in Kyushu, Japan (d. 1719)[1][2]
Patrick Gass, sergeant of Lewis and Clark Expedition, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1870)
Karl Freiherr von Müffling, Prussian field marshal, born in Halle, Duchy of Magdeburg, Holy Roman Empire (now Germany) (d. 1851)
Robert Clark, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from New York (1819-21), born in Washington County, New York (d. 1837)
John Marston, U.S. Navy officer, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1885)
Samuel Cooper, American General (Confederate Army), born in New Hackensack, Dutchess County, New York (d. 1876)
Harriet Martineau, British controversial journalist, political economist, abolitionist and life-long feminist, born in Norwich, Norfolk, England (d. 1876)
John Roebling, German-American civil engineer (Brooklyn Bridge), born in Mühlhausen, Germany (d. 1869)[1]
Edmond Hébert, French geologist, born in Villefargau, Yonne, France (d. 1890)
Charles Kingsley [Parson Lot], English vicar and historian (Westward Ho!), born in Holne Vicarage, England (d. 1875)
Henry Moses Judah, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Snow Hill, Maryland (d. 1866)
John Cook, American bugler (Union Army), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1910)
George Stevens, English jockey (record 5 Grand National wins), born in Cheltenham, England (d. 1871)
George Atzerodt, American conspirator who failed to attempt an assassination of Vice President Andrew Johnson on the night Abraham Lincoln was murdered, born in Dorna, German Confederation (d. 1865)
Rikard Nordraak, Norwegian composer, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1866)
Oliver Joseph Lodge, English physicist, early radio pioneer, born in Penkhull, England (d. 1940)
Achille Simonetti, Italian violinist and composer, born in Turin, Italy (d. 1928)
Jack Edwards, Australian cricket batsman (3 Tests, Victoria CA), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1911)
William Attewell, English cricket bowler (England in 10 Tests 1884-1891), born in Keyworth, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1927)
Sol White, American Baseball HOF executive (Philadelphia Giants 4 x consecutive black C'ships 1904–1907), born in Bellaire, Ohio (d. 1955)
Willem Landré, Dutch composer and music educator, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1948)
Sam De Grasse, Canadian actor, born in Bathurst, New Brunswick (d. 1953)
Narciso Garay, Panamanian composer, born in Panama (d. 1953)
Thomas C. Hart, U.S. admiral and commander (Pacific Fleet), born in Davison, Michigan (d. 1971)
James Oliver Curwood, American journalist and writer (Kazan the wolfdog), born in Owosso, Michigan (d. 1927)
Myrtle McAteer, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1900), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1952)
Werner Erich Josten, German composer (Jungle), born in Elberfeld, Germany (d. 1963)
Zygmunt Janiszewski, Polish mathematician, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1920)
Egon Schiele, Austrian painter and graphic artist, born in Tulln an der Donau, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1918)
Djuna Barnes, American author (Nightwood), born in Cornwall-on-Hudson, New York (d. 1982)
Ferdinand Schörner, German field marshal, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1973)
John Donald Robb, American composer, born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1989)
Kommer Kleijn, Dutch actor and radio play director (Tomorrow It Will Be Better), born in Hilversum, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Eugénie Brazier, French chef who was the 1st woman to earn 3 Michelin stars (1933), and the 1st person earn 3 Michelin stars for 2 restaurants, born in La Tranclière, Ain, France (d. 1977)
Alexandre Tansman, Polish composer (Dyptique), born in Łódź, Poland (d. 1986)
Anni Albers, German-American textile artist and printmaker (1st textile artist to exhibit solo at MOMA), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1994)
Amadeo Roldán, Cuban composer and violinist, born in Paris (d. 1939)
Betsy van Es, Flemish actress (Sonna), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1955)
(Raymond) Lee Morgan, American actor (Dungeons of Harrow; The Last of the Fast Guns), born in Stanford, Texas (d. 1967)
Hendrik Elias, Flemish historian and Mayor of Ghent (1941-44), born in Machelen, Belgium (d. 1973)
Emmett Hardy, American jazz musician, born in Gretna, Louisiana (d. 1925)
Bill Foster, American Baseball HOF pitcher (NgL All-Star 1933, 34; NgL World Series 1926, 27; Chicago American Giants), born in Calvert, Texas (d. 1978)
Eino Roiha, Finnish composer, born in Vyborg, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1955)
Ray Barbuti, American athlete (Olympic gold 400m, 400m relay 1928), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1988)
Sandro Penna, Italian poet, born in Perugia, Italy (d. 1977)
Giorgio Nataletti, Italian composer, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1972)
Alphonse Ouimet, Canadian television pioneer and president of the CBC, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1988)
Marina Semyonova, Russian ballerina, born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 2010)
Max Tailleur, Dutch adjudant to Prince Bernhard and Jewish humorist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1990)
Ronald Selby Wright, Scottish Church of Scotland minister, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1995)
Archie Bleyer, American orchestra leader (Arthur Godfrey), born in Queens, New York (d. 1989)
Mansel Thomas, Welsh composer, and conductor (BBC Welsh Orchestra, 1946-65), born in Tylorstown, Rhondda, Wales (d. 1986)
Bill Naughton [William John Francis Naughton], English playwright (Alfie), born in Ballyhaunis, Ireland (d. 1992)
Russell "Lucky" Hayden [Pate Lucid], American actor (Judge Roy Bean, Three Men from Texas), born in Chico, California (d. 1981)
Ben Frommer, Polish-American character actor (Scarface; Battlestar Galactica, F Troop), voice artist, and publicist, born in Poland (d. 1992)
Jean Victor Allard, Canadian army general, born in Sainte-Monique-de-Nicolet, Quebec (d. 1996)
Bill Kenny, American singer (The Bill Kenny Show), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2012)
Peter Lancelot Williams, English dance journalist, born in Burton Joyce, Nottingham (d. 1995)
William Lundigan, American actor (Climax, Pinky, They Asked for It), born in Syracuse, New York (d. 1975)
Christopher Mayhew, British politician, broadcaster, and writer (Dear Viewer; Time to Explain: An Autobiography), born in London, England (d. 1997)[1]
David Rockefeller, American CEO (Chase Manhattan Bank) and philanthropist, born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2017)
Irwin Allen, American film and television producer (Land of the Giants; The Poseidon Adventure), born in New York City (d. 1991)
Ivan Tors, Hungarian TV producer (Sea Hunt, Flipper), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1983)
Raúl Héctor Castro, Mexican American lawyer, judge, and diplomat (Ambassador to El Salvador, Bolivia, and Argentina), born in Cananea, Sonora, Mexico (d. 2015)
Priscilla Lane, American actress (Arsenic & Old Lace), born in Indianola, Iowa (d. 1995)
Samuel Z. Arkoff, American film producer and co-founder of American International Pictures, born in Fort Dodge, Iowa (d. 2001)
Uta Hagen, German-American actress and theatre practitioner (Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?), born in Göttingen, Germany (d. 2004)
Dave Berg, American cartoonist (The Lighter Side of...), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2002)
James "Jim" Siedow, American actor (The Texas Chain Saw Massacre), born in Cheyenne, Wyoming (d. 2003)
Peter Jones, British actor (Chariots of Fire; From a Bird's Eye View), born in Wem, Shropshire, England (d. 2000)
William Woodward Jr., American businessman and horse breeder (Hanover National Bank), born in Oyster Bay, New York (d. 1955)
Christopher Derrick, British writer, born in Hungerford, Berkshire, England (d. 2007)
H. C. Artmann, Austrian writer, born in Vienna (d. 2000)
James Archibald Houston, Canadian author and filmmaker (Tikta'Liktak), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2005)
Johan Witteveen, Dutch economist, Minister of Finance (1963–65; 1967–71) and managing director of the IMF (1973-78), born in Zeist, Netherlands (d. 2019)
Leif Thybo, Danish composer and organist, born in Holstebro, Denmark (d. 2001)
Marta Pan, Hungarian-French sculptor (Great spiral), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2008)
Jackie Pallo, English professional wrestler, born in Islington, Greater London, England (d. 2006)
Henry Slesar, American writer and playwright (The Edge of Night), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2002)
Timir Pinegin, Russian sailor (Olympic gold 1960), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 2013)

Bob Davis, Australian football forward (All Australian captain 1958; VFL Premiership 1951, 52 Geelong FC), coach (VFL Premiership 1963 Geelong FC) and broadcaster (7 Network), born in Ballarat, Australia (d. 2011)
Michael Wishart, British figurative painter, born in London (d. 1996)
